A goals-based approach to investing and
philanthropy is built on the recognition that
capital is a means, not an end. In traditional
investing, this challenges the assumption that
maximizing wealth is the ultimate objective.
Instead, it recognizes that capital is merely
an instrument; the true goal is the intrinsic
value—such as financial security or a life of
well-being—that this capital can help achieve.
This distinction is increasingly central well
beyond private wealth, including in institutional
investing and public policy.
It is an equally crucial distinction for impact
investing and philanthropy. Without clear goals,
investors risk merely funding activities rather
than real

ly central well
beyond private wealth, including in institutional
investing and public policy.
It is an equally crucial distinction for impact
investing and philanthropy. Without clear goals,
investors risk merely funding activities rather
than real progress. By bringing the rigorous
focus of a goals-based approach to their impact
allocations, investors can move from simply
“doing good”to generating specific, meaningful
improvements in the world
This guide makes the case that before
diving into detailed strategies, structures, or
investments, it is crucial to clarify what you
are trying to accomplish. While this may seem
obvious, our experience working with hundreds
of investors shows that unclear or conflicting
goals often lead to disappointing results and
wasted time.
Drawing on insights from academic research
and the real-world experiences of investors
and their families, this guide focuses on the
work necessary to move beyond inherited
assumptions and focus on what actually
matters to you. Clarifying your goals sets the
stage for deploying your resources to more
effectively achieve all your goals—whether
financial, personal, or about generating a
positive impact on the world.

About this guide
CENTER FOR SUSTAINABLE FINANCE
AND PRIVATE WEALTH (CSP)
Spun out of Harvard University
in 2017, CSP is a vibrant network
of wealth holders, researchers,
educators, and entrepreneurs
committed to making private
wealth a force for good. Our
research and training empower
private investors, philanthropists,
inheritors, and their advisors to
achieve greater and more systemic
environmental and social impact
using all their forms of capital.
We operate globally with teams
based in the U.S., Switzerland, and
Singapore. Our academic home is
at the University of St.Gallen (HSG)
in Switzerland (Competence Center
for Sustainable Finance and Private
Wealth), working closely with the
MIT Sloan School of Management
Sustainability Initiative and the
Stanford Center on Philanthropy
and Civil Society.
MIT SLOAN SUSTAINABILITY
INITIATIVE
The MIT Sloan Sustainability
Initiative is a research and education
initiative committed to advancing
systems change for an equitable
and sustainable world. We created
the Sustainability Initiative to
empower forward-thinking people
to turn their sustainability ideas into
action. Positioned at the intersection
of business and sustainability, we
provide a collaborative environment
for students, alumni, faculty,
researchers, private-sector partners,
and policymakers to work together
and fuel sustaina

STANFORD PACS
Stanford PACS is a global,
interdisciplinary research center
at Stanford University and the
publisher of Stanford Social
Innovation Review, connecting
scholars and practitioners to
strengthen philanthropy, civil
society, and social innovation.
IMPACT FRONTIERS
Impact Frontiers helps investors
integrate impact alongside financial
risk and return in real investment
decisions. As a successor
organization to the Impact
Management Project, it convenes
peer-learning communities for
investors and facilitates consensus-
building on areas of impact
management not addressed by
existing standards, frameworks, or
guidance.

This guide emerged from a simple
observation: many investors struggle
to make progress on their social and
environmental impact goals (or their
financial goals) simply because they
haven't clearly defined them.
While many investors and philanthropists
are driven to make a difference, we
have observed that they often feel
overwhelmed and unsure where to start.
What they need isn't another set of rigid
prescriptions. This guide is built on a
simple premise:

While many investors and philanthropists
are driven to make a difference, we
have observed that they often feel
overwhelmed and unsure where to start.
What they need isn't another set of rigid
prescriptions. This guide is built on a
simple premise: investors need to give
themselves permission to clarify their
goals. Doing so is valuable whether you
are just starting out or feeling stuck in
a strategic rut. Indeed, if your current
strategy feels stale or reactive, revisiting
your goals can quickly unlock new energy.
The insights shared here don't tell you
what goals to pursue or how much
to prioritize each one. While your
specific priorities may be unique, the
fundamental challenges of balancing
multiple objectives and making coherent
decisions in a complex, dynamic world
are universal. We offer practical steps
you can apply again and again to
sharpen your thinking throughout your
journey, helping you to better shape your
investment and philanthropic strategies.
Foreword
Investor’s Guide
to Goals-based Investing
and Philanthropy
Traditional approaches often begin
by narrowing interests to a few “focus
areas.” Yet investors inherently juggle
multiple financial and impact goals.
When these are forced together, depth
is often lost. For example, an investor
might attempt to satisfy a deep desire
for impact with a generic ESG screen.
To avoid this, we advocate for focused
structures

y juggle
multiple financial and impact goals.
When these are forced together, depth
is often lost. For example, an investor
might attempt to satisfy a deep desire
for impact with a generic ESG screen.
To avoid this, we advocate for focused
structures—dedicated allocations of
resources—that enable you to pursue
multiple goals in a coherent manner.
This guide focuses on essential
groundwork: clarifying your various
goals, understanding how different
opportunities serve those goals, and
organizing your resources to best reach
them while managing the inevitable
complexities. All investors approach
these steps with different values and
perspectives; however, working through
them allows you to move beyond
inherited assumptions and deploy your
capital more purposefully.
The world needs diverse approaches to
solving its problems. The most exciting
possibility isn't that we all adopt the
same approach to impact, but that we
each find our most effective way to
contribute. We hope this guide helps you
navigate that path with confidence.

Identify your goals 	Plan your approach
Start with foundational work. Every investor
has a multitude of goals, constraints, and
opportunities (Insight 1). It is important to clarify
your goals to bring structure to this complexity:
understand that each goal acts as a unique
lens through which you evaluate opportunities
(Insight 2). A deeper understanding of your own
goals also enables you to better grasp other
stakeholders’ perspectives and coordinate with
others to achieve win-win results.
The clarity that comes from identifying your
goals helps you ensure coherence in your
decision-making, so you don’t leave value
on the table (Insight 3

tter grasp other
stakeholders’ perspectives and coordinate with
others to achieve win-win results.
The clarity that comes from identifying your
goals helps you ensure coherence in your
decision-making, so you don’t leave value
on the table (Insight 3). You can support this
coherence by creating focused structures:
dedicated allocations of capital and resources
toward specific priorities (Insight 4). Ultimately,
a coherent approach gives you the best possible
chance of achieving your goals.
1 	3	2 	4
Many investors, even highly successful ones,
struggle to make progress on their financial and
impact goals because they lack a clear sense of
what they are really trying to accomplish. This
is increasingly recognized well beyond private
wealth, including in institutional investing and
public policy.
Goals-based investing and philanthropy means
investing more time upfront to clarify your real
goals, and then using them to guide decisions
over time. This guide explains why that
discipline matters, and offers practical insights
for both advanced investors refining their
strategy and beginners charting their course.

Max Zeller studied mechanical
engineering and felt a general pull
toward clean tech, but he soon realized
that a vague interest wasn't enough
to drive real change. He narrowed his
focus relentlessly until he landed on a
single, specific thesis: carbon removal.
This clarity allowed him to bypass
generalist distractions and build a
specialized venture firm, Carbon
Removal Partners.
“It felt so logical to me that this
is the direction we need to go.

Max’s journey illustrates that focusing

Dario Guarita Neto helped his family
steer the Maria Cecilia Souto Vidigal
Foundation through a classic challenge.
The foundation began with a focus
on hematology, after a child in the
family died of leukemia. Over time, as
treatments improved, they faced a key
question: was the goal impact in one
medical area, or helping children more
broadly. The family aligned on the
broader goal and chose early childhood
development as their next focus.
“W e changed how we thought
about the foundation’s goal,
and that changed what we could do
together.”
Dario’s family story shows that
alignment isn’t about everyone seeing
the world in the same way. It’s about
finding shared goals that let groups
coordinate their resources effectively.

Julie Engelhorn manages a diverse
set of goals by maintaining distinct,
focused structures for each, including
one for catalytic investing, one for
systemic investing, an allocation to high-
leverage philanthropy, and a personal
bucket for her family and regenerative
farming projects.
“I realized I didn't need one
strategy to rule them all. I
needed different vehicles for
different kinds of impact, and
the freedom to manage them
differently.”
Julie’s approach illustrates that the
key to managing complexity is often
separation: giving yourself permission
to pursue different goals in different
mental or formal buckets.

Hanson Gong began his journey
inspired simply by Tikkun Olam (the
concept of repairing the world), but
as he engaged deeper, he realized
that funding one company at a time
often failed to address root causes. He
realized lasting impact would require
shifting the system itself. This goal led
him to systemic investing.
“T o repair the world, you have to
understand how it is broken.
Systemic investing allows us to
target the root causes rather than
just treating the symptoms.”
Hanson’s evolution highlights that
advanced strategies are not just
intellectual exercises; they are the
necessary tools for investors whose
goals have moved beyond symptoms
to roots.
Betsy and Jesse Fink were early
pioneers in climate change investing
through MissionPoint Capital, but as
capital flooded into renewable energy,
they realized their contribution was
shrinking. Around the same time, Betsy’s
experience with farming led them to
food waste, a then-neglected driver
of emissions that was receiving little
attention. They co-founded ReFED to
build the field from scratch.
“W e need to go where the capital
isn't. Food waste was where
climate change was 15 years before,
a massive opportunity that everyone
was overlooking.”
The Finks’ story is a reminder that a
goal is not a lifetime sentence. When
the world changes or you succeed, the
best move is often to declare victory and

INSIGHT 	1 	2 	3 	5	4 	6
Clear goals are
foundational
As Paul Brest and Hal Harvey argue
in their book Money Well Spent, a
smart strategy built on clear goals
is central to generating significant
positive impact.1
“If one does not know to
which port one is sailing,
no wind is favorable.”
— Seneca
This isn't just motivational fluff;
it's backed by decades of research
showing that specific, challenging
goals significantly boost performance
compared to vague intentions like “doing
your best.”2
This guide takes its title from “goals-
based investing.”3 This means managing
wealth not simply to max

decades of research
showing that specific, challenging
goals significantly boost performance
compared to vague intentions like “doing
your best.”2
This guide takes its title from “goals-
based investing.”3 This means managing
wealth not simply to maximize returns
(e.g., a certain amount in your account),
but to achieve real-world outcomes that
contribute to an investor's well-being
(e.g., a certain lifestyle, after accounting
for inflation). For example, MIT’s Robert
Merton makes this point in retirement
planning, and Stanford’s John Cochrane
makes it for long-horizon investors, by
focusing on what a portfolio can pay for
over time.
The idea of a goals-based approach also
applies to more philanthropic pursuits.
It comes down to one question: “What
are the ultimate outcomes I am trying to
achieve with my capital?
The power of specific goals
Here, we use “goals” as an umbrella
term for everything from high-level
missions to precise, tactical targets.
At any level, a goal is only useful if
it is specific enough to guide your
choices when trade-offs arise. Sport is
the clearest example: structured goal
setting improves performance.4
Clarity helps under pressure
For investors, the challenge isn't just
the multitude of potential goals to
choose from; it's also the constant
barrage of external pressures. Advisors
may push for a simplistic focus on a
single metric, while peers advocate

rity helps under pressure
For investors, the challenge isn't just
the multitude of potential goals to
choose from; it's also the constant
barrage of external pressures. Advisors
may push for a simplistic focus on a
single metric, while peers advocate
for their own favored causes. Both
can feel incomplete because they
fail to account for an investor's core
identity, moral commitments, and
relationships.5 In the face of these
pressures, a clear make it easier to
evaluate advice and peer input.

Dimensions of goals

Dimensions of Alice's goals
Illustrated story:
Alice
Figure 2 provides a framework to help
clarify and understand any goal. While
it draws inspiration from frameworks
like Impact Frontiers’ five dimensions
of impact, originally developed by the
Impact Management Project (IMP), it is
uniquely focused on goals.
To illustrate, imagine Alice, an investor
who has just inherited a significant
fortune. Table 1 on the next page shows
three of her core goals mapped to the
dimensions.
Three of these dimensions deserve
special attention. The WHY dimension
clarifies a goal's purpose: is it an end in
itself (intrinsic) or a means to a larger end
(instrumental)?
While your intrinsic goals may be
ambitious and long-term, success often
requires breaking them down. Effective
investors set instrumental goals:
smaller, manageable targets that serve
as stepping stones toward the larger
objective. Yet, these instrumental goals
are not ends in themselves, and this
distinction is important to k

investors set instrumental goals:
smaller, manageable targets that serve
as stepping stones toward the larger
objective. Yet, these instrumental goals
are not ends in themselves, and this
distinction is important to keep in mind.
The RISK dimension captures the level
of certainty required: is the goal essential
(must be achieved) or aspirational
(ambitious, but failure can be tolerated)?
Finally, the HOW MUCH dimension
defines the nature of the goal. Is
it satisficing (aiming for 'enough')
or maximizing (aiming for the best
possible)?
Ultimately, treat your goals as
hypotheses along these dimensions—
provisional yet action-guiding views on
what is valuable to you. They should
evolve as you learn and grow.

For many investors, the default financial
goal is simply “more.” This vague aim,
often reinforced by advisors, can create
issues both personally and systemically.
On a personal level, it can create
persistent anxiety about never having
enough. At a systemic level, a relentless
focus on accumulation creates its own
set of complex challenges.
The Enough Project, founded by Brent
Kessel and Robert Boogaard, offers a
powerful alternative by asking a different
question: “How much is enough?”
Their process encourages a thoughtful
exploration of wealth, impact, and well-
being, centered on three core elements:
Having enough
This involves a structured process of
working out how much you actually
need for your desired lifestyle and loved
ones. By replacing the drive for endless
accumulation with a concrete number,
you can gain financial clarity and a
genuine sense of security.
Doing enough
Once you define “enough,” you can
unlock your “surplus”. This allows
resources that aren't required for your
enough goal to be deployed in line with
the world's needs, enabling a meaningful
contribution to tackling pressing
problems.

The power of moral ambition
Research shows that setting specific,
challenging goals drives superior
performance. This insight becomes
particularly relevant when considering
the scale of ambition in the world of
impact.
Historian Rutger Bregman champions
the idea of “moral ambition.” He
contrasts the common drive for personal
success or wealth with an ambition that
seeks to apply skills and resources to
tackling the world’s biggest problems.
It’s about treating “doing good” with the
same intensity and strategic rigor as
personal achievement.
This concept ties directly into the
distinction between essential and
aspirational goals. While you might set
clear and

It’s about treating “doing good” with the
same intensity and strategic rigor as
personal achievement.
This concept ties directly into the
distinction between essential and
aspirational goals. While you might set
clear and challenging essential goals for
your financial security, the idea of moral
ambition encourages you to set equally

It pushes you to think bigger about your

What is an “impact goal”?
This guide’s framework can help
you with any type of goal, but it’s
particularly useful for investors looking
to create positive change. So, what
defines an “impact goal”?
For our purposes, an impact goal is
simply any goal focused primarily
on “others”—such as a community,
society, or the planet. It can be an
intrinsic goal (where the outcome
for others is the end in itself) or an
instrumental goal that supports such
intrinsic aims.
This is a definition, not a claim about
which goals are better. Still, it has
profound implications. Investors who
prioritize such goals tend to focus on
several key principles:
Contribution: When the goal is to
benefit others, the key question
becomes: “Did my actions create a real
positive change that wouldn’t have
happened otherwise?” The attribution
for that change becomes secondary to
whether the change actually occurred.
Systems Awareness: Helping othe

benefit others, the key question
becomes: “Did my actions create a real
positive change that wouldn’t have
happened otherwise?” The attribution
for that change becomes secondary to
whether the change actually occurred.
Systems Awareness: Helping others
solve problems often requires
understanding the complex system in
which they exist, including potential
unintended consequences. This reduces
the risk of unintended harm and helps
identify the highest-leverage ways to
help.
Stakeholder Perspective: Finally, a
focus on outcomes for others requires
you to understand what those “others”
actually value. This means engaging with
stakeholders to grasp their values and
appetite for risk, ensuring your strategy
aligns with their needs rather than just
your assumptions.
As noted earlier, specific goals drive
superior performance. However,
applying this logic to impact is inherently
challenging. Unlike a race time or a bank
balance, impact is multi-dimensional
and often difficult to assess. Managing
impact well demands a high level of self-
awareness and a willingness to iterate.
See Insight 6 for a discussion of how
goals evolve.

Diversity in perspectives doesn’t
have to be a problem; it can be a
source of strength. As the old parable
reminds us, when several blind men try
to describe an elephant, each has a valid
but incomplete perspective. Complex
social and environmental problems
are the same; they are too vast to be
understood through a single lens. The
world needs diverse perspectives to
solve its problems. The challenge, then,
is not to force everyone to see the world
the same way. It’s about recognizing
our own lenses and learning to engage
constructively with those who view the
world differently.

Navigating Gridlock
When a group of people with different
lenses tries to make a decision, the result
is often “gridlock”—either persistent
conflict or avoidance of the topic. In
their book Breaking Through Gridlock,
Jason Jay and Gabriel Grant argue that
the way forward isn't to quarrel over the
“facts” of an investment, but to engage
in structured conversations about the
underlying lenses.
Conversations get stuck when we
become attached to being “right” and
see others as “resistant.” The key is to
notice this attachment and separate
disagreements about goals from
disagreements about methods. Then,
ground yourself in what you truly want
(both progress on your goal and a
healthy relationship), and then shift to a
space of curiosity and inquiry.
From there, ask questions to understand
what the other person values, what their
goals are, and why those goals matter
to them. This shared understanding
creates a foundation for a creative
search for “win-win” options—new
investment ideas that can achieve both
sets of objectives, rather than assuming
a simple trade-off. Move from asking
'what are my goals and values?' to
exploring, 'what goals do I share with
a coalition strong enough to achieve
them together?'”

How to put this
insight into action
Breaking Alice and Bob's Gridlock
The problem lies in a subtle, unstated
difference in their underlying goals.
When they finally use the principles of
navigating gridlock to understand each
other's lenses, the true source of the
conflict is revealed:
Bob's goal is to help people who are
suffering now, and he prefers low-risk,
proven solutions with high-certainty
outcomes.
Alice's goal is to generate as much
impact as possible over the long run,
making her more open to higher-risk,
evidence-backed bets that promise
much larger, albeit less certain, payoffs.
This shared understanding breaks the
stalemate. They realize their conflict
isn't about facts; it’s about their differing
views on the Time (WHEN) dimension.
Bob seeks immediate certainty, while
Alice focuses on future potential. Such
divergences can happen across any
dimension.
Moving forward
They agree on a practical next step:
rather than arguing over which approach
is “better,” they create distinct buckets
for each goal.

Moving forward
They agree on a practical next step:
rather than arguing over which approach
is “better,” they create distinct buckets
for each goal. In other situations, teams
might instead agree on a blended
strategy, run a time-boxed pilot, or
decide to prioritize one lens for now.
To balance and coordinate these buckets
in the long run, they also agree to

They recognize that while separation
works for now, finding shared ground
could unlock greater leverage in the

This illustrates how gridlock is rarely
resolved by arguing over data or
making assumptions about what
others are thinking. It is resolved by
acknowledging how people’s lenses
differ and then choosing a structure
that fits, sometimes a clear decision,
sometimes a compromise, and
sometimes distinct buckets. Their
chosen resolution moves them from
conflict to action.

focused bucket for “Future Health
Innovation,” she faces a new challenge:
how does she coherently choose between
a venture capital investment and a
research grant to achieve that goal?

Choosing which goals to prioritize is a personal,
often moral, decision. However, once you select
your goals, you can assess your actions against
a clear standard: coherence. While no one can
dictate your values, anyone can assess if your
decisions align with them.
What “coherence” means
Even if you're uncertain about your
values, or how to act on them, you
can control whether your decisions fit
together and reflect the same values.
You can debate goals on moral grounds,
but coherence is about whether your
actions match the goals you claim to
have. If your choices are a muddled
reflection of your priorities, they won’t
effectively represent what you care
about. In that case, you risk leaving
value on the table according to your
own criteria.

less coherent than they think, often for
understandable reasons: committees,
legacy commitments, and the complexity

Trade-offs are often uncomfortable,
which is why many investors keep their
goals vague or treat each investment
as a stand-alone decision. But ignoring
trade-offs doesn’t make them vanish; it
only leads to poor management.
Even an investor who believes they
only fund “win-win” opportunities
cannot escape this reality. Given two
excellent investments, they still have
to choose which one is the best use of
capital.

devoted to a single impact goal, there

The Gates Foundation offers a powerful

how to manage the latter
trade-off in practice.9 As documented
in the Stanford Social Innovation Review,
when considering a Program-Related
Investment (PRI) in a high-impact,

essentially ask: “Is the impact we expect

The investment team defines the

an investment compared to more

grantmaking team assesses whether
this expected cost is a wise use of the
foundation's capital. If so, they fund the
risk share from their budget. This isn't
just an accounting trick; it’s a way to make
investment decisions that consistently
reflect their grant-making strategy.

The “risk share” approach relates to a
growing strategic tool: impact valuation.
This is the practice of making the
importance of an investment's impacts
clear by expressing them in a common
metric. This helps manage trade-offs and
guide decision-making.
In the case of the Gates Foundation,
an investment being worth its “risk
share” implies that its impact is at
least as important as the outcomes
the foundation could generate with an
alternative grant.

more coherent isn’t enough. You may
need to separate the goals entirely.

goals (Insight 4).

The benefits of focus
The purpose of focused structures
is twofold: to manage conflicts and
harness synergies. By separating
unrelated goals (like financial security
and global health), what often
feels like a constant juggling act
can become occasional, high-level
decisions.
Grouping related goals creates a
dedicated space to cultivate potential
synergies. The resulting sense of
harmony, where conflicts between
goals are managed and outweighed
by strong synergies, has been shown
to be a strong predictor of a goal-
setter's persistence, performance,
and well-being.11

DEFINING FOCUSED STRUCTURES
A focused structure is an active,
dedicated allocation of capital, resources,
and activities toward a specific set of
goals. Effective structures share several
key features:
⁕ They have defined priorities. Each
structure has clear priorities that allow
for a coherent competition for capital
within it.
⁕ Serve related goals. Goals whose
activities interact—whether through
conflict or synergy—are best managed
together.
⁕ Separate out unrelated goals. Goals
that are unrelated are best separated
to allow for focused execution in
parallel.
⁕ Formal or informal. A structure can
be a distinct legal entity, like a trust
or foundation, or simply a “mental
account.”
Balancing multiple goals at once can be
challenging and often unproductive. We all
have limited attention and decision-making
capacity (“bounded rationality”). A general
solution is to create focused structures.

isn't just a feeling; research shows
that philanthropy can be a source of
significant well-being for many people.13
A core problem with using wealth as
a measure of success is that there are
always wealthier people, which can
leave you feeling dissatisfied. But if you
use helping others as a measure of
success, then it is natural to applaud and
appreciate those who do even more.
This creates a positive feedback loop: the
act of contributing reinforces your sense
of 'Being Enough' (Insight 1), which in
turn unlocks an awareness of additional
opportunities to contribute.
The Knowledge Illusion:
why we never think alone
The humility required for this journey
rests on a simple truth: we all know
far less than we think we do. In their
book The Knowledge Illusion, cognitive
scientists Steven Sloman and Philip
Fernbach show that human intelligence
is fundamentally collective.14 We thrive
by drawing on the expertise and
perspectives of others.
This humility brings us full circle to
Insights 1 and 2. Because our knowledge
is collective, our goals evolve best when
we work with other stakeholders to
clarify shared goals and manage our
differences. True stewardship isn't about
knowing everything yourself; it's about
building a learning system that evolves

The Impact Frontier
PRELIMINARY DRAFT
Please do not cite or circulate without permission
JONATHAN HARRIS∗
January 31, 2026
ABSTRACT
Asset pricing usually takes firms’ demand for capital as given, while corporate finance does
the same for investors’ supply of capital. In a unified model, what real effects can investor
portfolio tilts have on climate- and nature-related externalities over multi-year horizons? This
paper develops such an equilibrium model and constructs the Impact Frontier: the Pareto frontier
between certainty-equivalent returns and the expected effect on an externality. Portfolios on the
impact frontier are obtained by allocating to an impact-tilt portfolio defined by additionality-
adjusted externality scores—raw scores (illustrated by ESG ratings) multiplied by a matrix
derived from demand- and supply-side elasticities. Calibrated to U.S. equity data, the impact
frontier differs materially from the ESG-efficient frontier; portfolios based only on raw scores
can lie far below the frontier and even yield negative expected impact.

1 Introduction
A central question in climate and nature finance is whether, and through which mechanisms, investor
portfolio choices can have real effects on firms’ activities and the associated externalities. A common
modeling divide is that asset pricing often takes firms’ demand for capital as given, while corporate
finance takes investors’ supply of capital as given.1 This paper develops a unified equilibrium
model of these two sides and uses it to construct the Impact Frontier: the Pareto frontier between
certainty-equivalent returns and the expected effect on an externality.
The paper focuses on the capital-allocation channel of portfolio tilts and how they transmit into
installed capital and an externality; related channels such as shareholder engagement and governance
are complementary mechanisms but are not the focus of the model. In this framework, “investor
impact” is defined as the expected change in the equilibrium level of an aggregate externality caused
by a marginal shift in an investor’s supply schedule (Brest, Gilson, and Wolfson, 2018; K ¨olbel et al.,
2020). A key challenge is that the same portfolio tilt can have very different real effects across firms
because the pass-through from investor supply of capital to firm scale depends on both the slope of
investor supply and the slope of firms’ demand for

. A key challenge is that the same portfolio tilt can have very different real effects across firms
because the pass-through from investor supply of capital to firm scale depends on both the slope of
investor supply and the slope of firms’ demand for capital.
The paper’s first contribution is to provide a tractable microfounded supply-and-demand system
in which these slopes (and hence real effects) are allowed to vary in the cross section. The central
result is that portfolios designed to maximize investor impact for a given certainty-equivalent return
objective will generally differ from portfolios that tilt only on raw externality scores—for example,
firm-level ESG ratings. In particular, ignoring heterogeneity in pass-through can move an investor
away from the impact frontier and, in calibrated examples, can even imply negative investor impact
despite tilting toward firms with higher raw scores.

Using the estimated system, I construct the impact frontier and compare it to an “ESG-efficient”
frontier (Pedersen, Fitzgibbons, and Pomorski, 2021) that tilts only on raw scores, as well as

pass-through and cross-firm interactions are taken into account.

impact on climate- and nature-related externalities requires jointly modeling investors’ supply of
capital and firms’ demand for capital, especially when the target is real effects over multi-year
horizons with more elastic supply and demand.
This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the supply-and-demand system and its
equilibrium when impact preferences are turned off. Section 3 introduces the externality, defines
investor impact, and develops the impact frontier when impact preferences are turned on. Section
4 estimates the model, and Section 5 uses the estimates to construct contribution multipliers and
impact frontiers. Section 6 discusses limitations and extensions and Section 7 concludes.

2.3 Equilibrium Supply and Demand
The market clears at the set of asset prices where aggregate demand equals aggregate supply. Similar
to Haddad, Huebner, and Loualiche (2021), investors are sophisticated and strategically update their
supply curves to be optimal for their own expected utility given their beliefs about aggregate demand
and supply5. This can be interpreted as firms and investors submitting supply and demand schedules
to a neutral market maker, who then clears the market. This is an important but subtle shift from
standard models, because in a supply and demand system agents don’t have direct control over how
much they are able to sell or buy, but only on the schedule they offer to the market.
In general, the equilibrium may not be unique or exist and determining the optimal investor
supply may be complex (e.g., for investors that are large or have concentrated initial holdings).

4 Empirical Estimation
This section details the empirical estimation of the supply and demand system. The empirical
strategy builds on the joint estimation of supply and demand curves pioneered by BCJ while
incorporating Merton (1987)’s insights on heterogeneous investor opportunity sets. Section 4.1
describes the data, sample construction, and key variables. Section 4.2 presents the econometric

Least Squares (OLS), Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS), and the preferred Three-Stage Least Squares
(3SLS) specifications.
4.1 Data and Variable Construction
My analysis utilizes data for U.S. stocks from the merged CSP-Compustat database covering the
period 1999-2024. Following BCJ, I construct an unbalanced panel focusing on cross-sections at
year-end 2004, 2009, 2014, and 2019. Standard filters are applied: I exclude financial firms (SIC
6000-6999) and utility firms (SIC 4900-4949) and require at least one year of return data. Firm-level
externality-score proxies are sourced from MSCI and are used throughout as raw externality scores
for illustration (e.g., MSCI pillar scores). The construction of key variables largely follows BCJ,
unless otherwise noted.

4.3 Estimation Results
The system is estimated using OLS, 2SLS, and 3SLS. OLS estimates, while potentially biased due
to endogeneity, provide a baseline. 2SLS provides consistent estimates for each equation separately.
3SLS estimates the system jointly, leveraging the correlation between the equation residuals for
potentially greater efficiency.
Table II presents the OLS and 2SLS estimates for the core specifications. The OLS results
(Columns 1 and 2) show significant correlations, but the estimated slopes may be biased. For
instance, the OLS slope for log book equity growth in the supply equation is negative and significant,
while the OLS slopes for the risk quantities in the demand equation are significant but potentially
conflate supply and demand effects. The 2SLS results (Columns 3 and 4) apply the IV strategy.

In the firm demand equation, the coefficient on log book equity growth (∆D) is negative and
statistically significant. This negative slope suggests firms scale back growth plans when faced with
a higher cost of capital (required Sharpe ratio). The lagged Sharpe ratio remains a strong positive
predictor, indicating persistence.
In the investor supply-of-capital equation, the coefficient on external market correlation (∆S,sys)
is positive and highly significant. This confirms that investors require higher compensation
for systematic risk. The coefficients for the non-market risk interactions (∆S,nonmkt,k) decrease
exponentially from the smallest size group to the largest and all are statistically significant. Their
positive signs and decreasing magnitude with size strongly support the Merton (1987) hypothesis
that non-market risk is priced due to market segmentation, with the effect diminishing for larger,
more widely held firms. Results with alternative size groups, shown in Table IV, are qualitatively

These 3SLS results provide a set of statistically significant and theoretically coherent estimates
for the key supply and demand slopes. They form the basis for calculating the estimated contribution
multiplier matrix and analyzing the impact frontier in the subsequent sections. While the statistical
precision of some estimates is modest, the qualitative pattern of results is robust across specifications
and consistent with theoretical predictions. The primary contribution of this paper is identifying
the role of contribution multipliers in determining investor impact, rather than precisely estimating
specific asset pricing factors. The empirical results should be viewed as a first step toward quantifying
these relationships, with future research refining these estimates using more data and more elaborate
empirical specifications.

4.4 Observed Supply Shifts for Raw Externality-Score Proxies
Before constructing the impact frontier, it is useful to benchmark the magnitude of investor supply
shifts associated with commonly used sustainability-related scores. This provides context for
interpreting the impact frontier results in the following section, as it indicates where on the frontier
investors might seek to operate in practice.
Empirical estimates of the supply shifts associated with popular sustainability-related metrics
are summarized in Table VI and discussed below. To enable comparison between results from
different studies, the estimates are presented as implied ‘impact returns’ for the average firm in my
model. Furthermore, given a supply coefficient for the overall market, it must be the case that some
investors have even larger supply coefficients. To illustrate this in a way that is consistent across the
metrics examined in this section, I also report results for supply coefficients that are five times larger.

The substantial cross-sectional variation in the additionality multipliers challenges the conven-
tional wisdom that public market investors have limited ability to generate impact, at least at the
five year horizons examined here. Some firms indeed have low multipliers (below 10%) and all the
more so for the results based on IV. However, some firms have much higher multipliers, suggesting
significant heterogeneity in impact potential across firms. This variation is not strongly correlated
with traditional firm characteristics, underscoring the need for explicit estimation of contribution
multipliers rather than relying on simple proxies. In particular, the additionality multipliers have
close to zero correlation with the raw externality-score proxies (illustrated here by ESG scores) for a
variety of specifications.
5.2 Impact frontiers
To construct the impact and ESG-efficient frontiers, I use the social score (from BCJ) as a
representative raw externality-score proxy. To define expected financial performance, I use the value
of the investor’s financial utility from Equation 2, per unit portfolio value, as the risk-adjusted excess
return (i.e., a certainty-equivalent return). This results in more robust and interpretable optimal
portfolios than defining the frontier based on nonlinear metrics like portfolio Sharpe ratios.

The figures show that the cross-sectional heterogeneity in the contribution multipliers drives a
wedge between the impact frontier and the ESG-efficient frontier. While the impact frontier shows
the best possible return for a given level of investor impact, the ESG-efficient frontier represents
portfolios that optimize for ESG scores without accounting for the heterogeneity in contribution.
Because the ESG-frontier is not optimizing for impact, but rather portfolio ESG scores, it does not
have to curve in the same direction as the impact frontier and indeed in both figures it does not.
The supply-shift coefficients in Table VI show that investors already exhibit meaningful score-
based tilts (illustrated here by ESG-related metrics). Comparing to the frontier charts highlights
that investors with average score-based tilts are operating in a region where the difference between
the impact frontier and the ESG-efficient frontier is economically significant and growing with the
strength of those preferences.

Note that the difference between the frontiers is driven purely by the heterogeneity of contribution
multipliers in the model. If there was no heterogeneity then the impact frontier and ESG-efficient
frontier would overlap. This is because if the contribution multipliers are the same for all firms then
not adjusting for contribution would merely change your demand coefficient and hence your chosen
position on the frontier–it wouldn’t change the shape of the frontier. What makes the impact frontier
different from the ESG-efficient frontier is that different firms present different opportunities for
investor impact per dollar and the impact-optimal policies take this into account.

6 Limitations and Potential Extensions
The model has several key features relative to conventional asset pricing models: the externality and
heterogeneous supply and demand elasticities unlock contribution multipliers and investor impact;
introducing a price of impact then traces out the impact frontier. While the current framework
makes several simplifying assumptions, the core insights about contribution multipliers and their
role in investing are likely to be robust to various model extensions. Yet, there are numerous natural
refinements a

ontier. While the current framework
makes several simplifying assumptions, the core insights about contribution multipliers and their
role in investing are likely to be robust to various model extensions. Yet, there are numerous natural
refinements and extensions that could be important.
First, it is a single period model, and the microfounded case is for an all-equity economy.
Throughout, the single period can be interpreted as a reduced-form representation of portfolio tilts
whose real effects materialize over multi-year horizons, consistent with the empirical design.
BCJ include debt in their model and, in their appendix, show how to extend the model to multiple
periods. As such, I don’t expect such extensions to be problematic. That is not to say such extensions
would not be valuable. In particular, I expect that the price of impact could be made endogenous
in a multi-period model, with investors balancing financial returns and managing the externality,
which will also affect their future welfare.
Other features that could be more explicitly studied within this framework include: the effect
of transaction costs, locally increasing returns to scale for selected firms, the ‘valley of death’
phenomena in some markets (i.e., as a lack of investor supply and an opportunity for investor impact),
firm manager preferences beyond shareholder value (e.g., for the externality, cash fl

7 Conclusion
By extending the supply and demand system of BCJ to incorporate an externality while maintaining
analytical tractability, this paper demonstrates how cross-sectional variation in supply and demand
elasticities drives a wedge between the impact frontier and frontiers constructed from raw score tilts
(e.g., the ESG-efficient frontier). This result highlights both the potential for meaningful investor
impacts in public markets over multi-year horizons and the risk that portfolios tilted only on raw
scores may generate little or even negative expected impact if contribution is ignored. The framework
is designed to speak directly to climate and nature finance questions in which real-economy effects
arise through changes in firms’ installed capital.
Asset returns, the impact of investor supply shifts, and the optimal portfolio choice of consequen-

ed to speak directly to climate and nature finance questions in which real-economy effects

Asset returns, the impact of investor supply shifts, and the optimal portfolio choice of consequen-
tialist investors all hinge on the aggregate supply and demand elasticities through the contribution
multiplier matrix. The off-diagonal elements of this matrix capture systemic effects where supply
shifts for one firm affect the equilibrium capital of other firms—effects that are entirely missed
by simplified approaches that do not assess impact or assume independent firm responses. Assets
characterized by higher supply elasticities and lower demand elasticities offer greater contribution
multipliers, making them more attractive opportunities for investor impact and, potentially, for
expected returns as the heterogeneous supply side means that non-market risks are priced.
The approach provided here offers a systematic way to address a central question about the
role of finance in society: how can investors most effectively allocate capital given the real-world
externalities of firms and financial markets? Future research can further refine the estimation of
contribution multipliers and explore how they vary across different market segments, time periods,
